sections.paragraph: (a “sunny place,” a “sunny mountain;” from the root צָהָה [in Thes. from צָיָה]; comp. Arab. صَهْوَةُ a fortress; the radical h is retained in Syriac and Arabic, in which the name is written صهيون, ܨܗܶܝܘܽܢ), pr.n. f. Zion, the higher and southern hill (not the northern, as Lightfoot thought; see especially Barhebr. Chron. p. 282, lin. 5), on which the city of Jerusalem was built. It included the more ancient part of the city, with the citadel and temple (Mount Moriah, on which the temple was built, being reckoned to Zion [separated by a narrow valley]); also called the city of David, 2Ch 5:2. Very often used by the prophets and poets for Jerusalem itself, Isa 2:3 8:18 10:24 33:14 , and its inhabitants (fein.), Isa 1:27 49:14 52:1 . Psa 97:8 Zep 3:16. They are also poetically called the daughter of Zion (see בַּת No. 5 ) בַּת צִיֹּון Isa 1:8 10:32 62:11 (also while in exile, Isa 40:9 Zec 2:11, 14 Zec 2:14); and יֹושֶׁבֶת צ׳ Zec 12:6 but בְּנֹות צִיֹּון are the women of Jerusalem, Isa 3:16, 17 4:3 . Once followed by a genit. Isa 60:14, צִיֹּון קְדֹושׁ יִשְׂרָאֵל “The Zion of the Holy One of Israel,” i.e. sacred to him.
